pi_items.source: Confirmed via learneconometrics.com/gretl/poe5/data/food.gdt - 40 observations of household food expenditure and income from Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition
Computed via OLS regression on 40 observations: food_exp = 83.416 + 10.210*income (R²=0.385). Exact match.
Computed via OLS regression on food.gdt dataset. Difference: 0.0004 (within rounding error).
Computed via OLS regression. Linear model explains 38.50% of variance in food expenditure.
Computed via OLS regression of ln(food_exp) on constant and ln(income). Actual coefficient is 3.9636, difference of 0.2736.
Computed via OLS regression of ln(food_exp) on ln(income). Income elasticity of food expenditure is 0.5559 (not 0.38). Difference of 0.1759 exceeds the tolerance of 0.10.
Computed via OLS regression of ln(food_exp) on constant and ln(income). Log-linear model R² is 0.4452, difference of 0.0852.

privileged_info_summary: The food.gdt dataset contains 40 observations of household food expenditure and weekly income from Principles of Econometrics, 5th Edition. The linear model food_exp = 83.42 + 10.21*income achieves R² = 0.385. All linear coefficients match the metadata exactly. However, the log-linear model ln(food_exp) = 3.964 + 0.556*ln(income) shows significant discrepancies: the intercept is 3.964 (claimed 3.69, difference 0.264), the elasticity is 0.556 (claimed 0.38, difference 0.176), and R² is 0.445 (claimed 0.36, difference 0.085). The income elasticity of food expenditure is approximately 0.556, not 0.38 as claimed in the metadata.
